question: Will renewables generate more than half of the world's electricity by 2035?

events.text: Dossier's in the channel — numbers first, vibes later. Baseline: renewables were roughly 30% of global electricity in 2023, hydro the biggest slice — Ember's Global Electricity Review and the IEA agree there. Trajectory: IEA Renewables 2024 has the main case approaching 46-50% of generation by 2030, driven mostly by China's buildout. Costs: the IEA's WEO 2020 called utility solar the cheapest electricity in history, and BloombergNEF has battery packs down about 90% since 2010. Constraints: LBNL's Queued Up report documents years-long interconnection queues in the US, transmission permitting is the repeat offender across Western markets, and China's UHV lines are the standing exception. New variable: IEA's Electricity 2024 flags data-centre load as the fastest-moving demand line. Thinnest part of the file: everything past 2030 is scenario, not data — and 2030-to-2035 is exactly the stretch this question lives in.

Look, the trendline is doing the arguing for me — I could end my screen share right here. The IEA's World Energy Outlook called solar the cheapest electricity in history back in 2020, and cost curves like that don't hit a hard stop because a committee got nervous. Every doubling of installed solar cuts costs roughly twenty percent, and deployment keeps compounding: China now installs more solar in a single year than the entire planet managed a decade ago. Wind rides a gentler version of the same curve. And storage, the classic blocker on every roadmap? It's riding shotgun on the EV curve — BloombergNEF's battery survey has pack prices down about ninety percent since 2010. Once the cheapest marginal kilowatt-hour is renewable, capital reallocates on its own; no treaty, no offsite required. From roughly thirty percent of generation today, one more decade of compounding clears half with room to spare. I'm at 85.

Good dossier — I checked its numbers against the primary sources over lunch, and they hold. Anchors first: renewables supplied roughly thirty percent of global electricity in 2023, per the IEA and Ember's Global Electricity Review, with hydro the largest slice and wind plus solar growing fastest. Solar is the fastest-growing power source in history — installed capacity has doubled about every three years lately — and IEA Renewables 2024's main case has renewables reaching forty-six to fifty percent of generation by 2030, driven overwhelmingly by China. So yes, the trend genuinely favors the optimist. Two caveats before anyone updates the roadmap. First, those are scenarios, sensitive to policy and grid investment — not forecasts of destiny. Second, the binding constraint has shifted from panel prices to interconnection queues, transmission, and storage: LBNL's Queued Up report has years' worth of US projects sitting in line for a grid connection. Crossing fifty by 2035 needs the 2030 trajectory to hold five more years without those bottlenecks biting hard. Plausible, not guaranteed. I'm at 62.

I've sat through this exact kickoff at two different utilities, so I'll take the other side: capacity is not generation, and press releases are not electrons. Solar runs at a fifteen-to-twenty-five percent capacity factor; a gigawatt of panels displaces far less coal than a gigawatt of anything dispatchable. Grids are the real story. LBNL's Queued Up shows US interconnection queues stretching for years, major transmission lines take a decade to permit, and storage beyond about four hours is still expensive at scale. Meanwhile the denominator is moving: demand in India, Southeast Asia, and now data centers — IEA's Electricity 2024 report, if you want the receipts — is growing fast, and much of that growth still lands on coal and gas, because that's what today's grid can absorb. Renewables can smash addition records and barely gain share. Thirty to above fifty in twelve years means rebuilding grids at wartime speed on every continent at once, and that's on nobody's roadmap I've seen. I'm at 38.

I'm going to be annoying for a second: everyone in this room is modeling the numerator while the denominator is sprinting. Read the question again — 'more than half' is a SHARE metric, which makes demand growth literally half the equation, and it's not on the agenda. IEA's Electricity 2024 report has data-center, AI, and crypto load growing fast enough to add a major economy's worth of demand within a few years, and that's before we electrify cars, heat, and half of industry. Hot take: renewables can win every auction, break every record, and still lose share to a growing pie. That's sprinting on a treadmill someone keeps speeding up. I'm not against the direction — the supply-side story is real. I'm against the framing where the target holds still while we compound toward it. Somebody should book a meeting about the denominator; until then, I'm at 40.

Cost curves don't build transmission lines. Panels could be free by Friday and it would change nothing for a project five years deep in an interconnection queue — that's LBNL's Queued Up data, not my mood. You also quietly slid from capacity to generation on that slide: the doubling you cite is installed gigawatts, and at solar's capacity factors the actual share of electricity moves far more slowly. And 'capital reallocates on its own' skips the part where the grid operator says no. Show me permitting reform and transmission buildout at global scale, not a learning curve, and I'll update.

Two corrections, offered politely. First, 'one more decade of compounding clears half' overstates what the data supports: capacity doublings don't map one-for-one onto generation share, because solar's capacity factor is a fraction of the fossil plants it displaces, and growth rates mathematically slow as the base gets large. Second, IEA Renewables 2024 has forty-six to fifty percent by 2030 in its main case — that supports 'likely,' not 'settled,' and the same report names grid and storage investment as the gating item. Your direction is right. Your certainty isn't. The honest read is a strong trend running into a real integration constraint.

You're describing Western permitting and projecting it onto the planet. Most of this buildout is happening in China, which stands up ultra-high-voltage transmission on timelines Brussels would need three offsites just to scope, and it's exporting that model along with the panels. BloombergNEF's survey has battery packs down about ninety percent in a decade, and grid-scale storage is now winning open capacity auctions on cost, full stop. As for your demand growth — it lands in sunny, fast-growing markets, which is exactly where solar-plus-storage is already the cheapest new build. Your bottleneck is real in Brussels and Sacramento. It is not the world.

I fact-checked the denominator take, and it's real but sized wrong. Global demand grows a few percent a year, and recent wind and solar additions have covered most of that growth — Ember's Global Electricity Review shows clean sources meeting the bulk of new demand. When new clean supply covers growth plus some displacement, share still climbs. Your treadmill slows the runner; it doesn't stop them. So the wildcard effect delays the crossing — it doesn't cancel it. One concession for the minutes: data-center load is the genuinely new variable. The IEA Electricity 2024 projections weren't in anyone's model two years ago. That's a line item, not a rewrite.

On the data, I concede — plainly, since you'll minute it anyway. Solar additions really have doubled about every three years, 2024 set another record, and I was underweighting how much of the thirty-to-fifty gap hydro and existing wind already cover: new wind and solar need maybe twenty points of share, not the whole distance. That moves me. But my objection was never the panels. It's whether grids and storage let those electrons reach the meter, and your own IEA citations name exactly that bottleneck. I'm updating upward, not surrendering.

Fine, the math checks — I ran it twice hoping it wouldn't. If clean builds cover demand growth plus some displacement, share grinds up even with my sprinting denominator. I'll move. But I want it minuted, per my last hot take: a couple of AI buildout years like 2024 and 2025 landing outside the forecast bands stretches the date, and IEA's own Electricity 2024 numbers treat the data-center curve as the least certain line in the deck. I'm conceding the direction and keeping the treadmill.

A fair concession, so let me size the residual: real but bounded. With hydro plus today's wind and solar as the base, the gap to fifty percent is roughly twenty points of share over twelve years — under two points a year, against a recent pace of about a point a year and accelerating, per IEA Renewables 2024. Queues are a genuine drag, but China and India are building transmission alongside generation — the UHV lines aren't hypothetical — and storage additions are setting records too. On the evidence, the bottleneck slows the date. I don't see it pushing the central case past 2035.

I'll trade a little certainty for the fact-checker's precision: the constraint is integration, not economics, and integration moves slower than cost curves. Fine. But the cost curve remains undefeated, and every bottleneck named in this meeting is a permitting-and-engineering ticket, not physics. Twenty points of share in twelve years, on an accelerating base, with storage getting cheap. Ship it. VOTE: Yes CONFIDENCE: 78

This debate narrowed to the right question: not whether renewables win, but whether grids, storage, and now demand growth keep pace with record additions. The IEA trajectory toward forty-six to fifty percent by 2030 leaves five years to close the remainder, and the required pace is close to what we already observe. The skeptic's grid concern and the wildcard's denominator each trim my confidence; neither flips the answer. VOTE: Probably yes CONFIDENCE: 72

I came in at 38 and I'm leaving at 62, and the solar deployment data earned every point of that — write it down, it doesn't happen often. The arithmetic is tighter than I assumed: twenty points of share, not fifty. My residual is storage duration and grid buildout outside China, which could stretch the crossing into the late 2030s. The date is the honest uncertainty. VOTE: Lean yes CONFIDENCE: 62

Lean yes — the supply math survived my best denominator attack, which is annoying, but that's the job. My residual is demand surprise, not supply: if the date slips, it'll be because the pie grew faster than the forecasts, and nobody books THAT meeting until it's already late. Keep one eye on the IEA's data-center numbers; the treadmill still has a speed dial. VOTE: Lean yes CONFIDENCE: 65

moderator.name: The Moderator
moderator.text: We're at time, and — someone check the calendar for a frost warning — this meeting actually reached a decision. We opened forty-seven points apart and closed within sixteen, which around here qualifies as a group hug. The Fact-Checker's anchors did the heavy lifting: thirty percent of generation in 2023, solar doubling roughly every three years, the IEA's main case at forty-six to fifty percent by 2030. The Optimist gave back some certainty once corrected on capacity versus generation; the Skeptic conceded the deployment data outright and reframed the fight as twenty points of share; the Wildcard's denominator attack got measured, absorbed, and — credit where due — updated on. All four now lean yes. What remains on the whiteboard is the date, and which bottleneck bites first: the grid buildout or the growing pie. ANSWER: Probably yes — deployment data, cost curves, and the IEA's own 2030 trajectory make renewables crossing half of global electricity by 2035 the central case. The live risks are grids and storage keeping pace, and demand from data centers and electrification outrunning the forecasts — either slips the date rather than flips the answer.

result.answer: Probably yes. Deployment data and cost trajectories make renewables crossing half of global electricity generation by 2035 the most likely outcome, with the IEA already projecting roughly 46-50 percent by 2030. The timing hinges on grid buildout and storage keeping pace with record solar and wind additions, and on demand growth from data centers and broader electrification not outrunning the forecasts.
result.dissent: All four agents lean yes; the residual split is over what could slip the crossing past 2035 — grid and storage buildout pace, per the skeptic, versus demand outrunning the forecasts, per the wildcard.
